1→import GameServer
     2→import Mathlib.Data.Fin.Basic
     3→
     4→/-! ## Documentation for disabled tactics and theorems
     5→
     6→These docs are needed by the GameServer when tactics/theorems are disabled.
     7→They are placed here so all level files can reference them.
     8→-/
     9→
    10→/-! ### NNG4-baseline tactics
    11→
    12→These tactics are assumed known from the Natural Number Game or
    13→equivalent. They are introduced in Level 1 so the game doesn't warn
    14→about missing introductions.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→/-- `exact e` closes the goal if `e` has exactly the type of the goal.
    18→
    19→## Syntax
    20→```
    21→exact expression
    22→```
    23→
    24→## When to use it
    25→When you have a term (variable, function application, constructor)
    26→whose type matches the goal exactly.
    27→-/
    28→TacticDoc exact
    29→
    30→/-- `rw [h]` rewrites the goal using an equation or iff `h`.
    31→
    32→## Syntax
    33→```
    34→rw [h]           -- rewrite the goal
    35→rw [h] at hyp    -- rewrite a hypothesis
    36→rw [← h]         -- rewrite right-to-left
    37→rw [h1, h2, h3]  -- chain multiple rewrites
    38→```
    39→
    40→## When to use it
    41→When you have an equation `h : a = b` or iff `h : P ↔ Q` and want
    42→to replace `a` with `b` (or `P` with `Q`) in the goal or a hypothesis.
    43→-/
    44→TacticDoc rw
    45→
    46→/-- `intro x` introduces a universally quantified variable or an
    47→antecedent of an implication into the context.
    48→
    49→## Syntax
    50→```
    51→intro x       -- introduce one variable
    52→intro x y z   -- introduce multiple variables
    53→intro h       -- introduce an implication hypothesis
    54→```
    55→
    56→## When to use it
    57→When the goal is `∀ x, P x` or `P → Q`.
    58→-/
    59→TacticDoc intro
    60→
    61→/-- `cases x with | constructor₁ => ... | constructor₂ => ...`
    62→performs case analysis on `x`, creating one subgoal per constructor.
    63→
    64→## Syntax
    65→```
    66→cases x with
    67→| constructor₁ args => tactic₁
    68→| constructor₂ args => tactic₂
    69→```
    70→
    71→## When to use it
    72→When you want to consider all possible forms of a value (e.g., natural
    73→numbers are `zero` or `succ n`, propositions with `∨` have `inl` and `inr`).
    74→-/
    75→TacticDoc cases
    76→
    77→/-- `have h := expr` introduces a new hypothesis `h` with value `expr`.
    78→
    79→## Syntax
    80→```
    81→have h := expr           -- Lean infers the type
    82→have h : type := expr    -- explicit type annotation
    83→have h : type := by ...  -- prove the hypothesis with tactics
    84→```
    85→
    86→## When to use it
    87→When you need an intermediate fact that isn't directly in your context.
    88→-/
    89→TacticDoc «have»
    90→
    91→/-- `use expr` provides a witness for an existential goal `∃ x, P x`.
    92→
    93→## Syntax
    94→```
    95→use expr
    96→```
    97→
    98→## When to use it
    99→When the goal is `∃ x, P x` and you know which `x` works.
   100→After `use expr`, the goal becomes `P expr`.
   101→-/
   102→TacticDoc use
   103→
   104→/-- `left` selects the left disjunct of a goal `P ∨ Q`.
   105→
   106→## Syntax
   107→```
   108→left
   109→```
   110→
   111→## When to use it
   112→When the goal is `P ∨ Q` and you want to prove `P`.
   113→-/
   114→TacticDoc left
   115→
   116→/-- `right` selects the right disjunct of a goal `P ∨ Q`.
   117→
   118→## Syntax
   119→```
   120→right
   121→```
   122→
   123→## When to use it
   124→When the goal is `P ∨ Q` and you want to prove `Q`.
   125→-/
   126→TacticDoc right
   127→
   128→/-- `rfl` closes a goal of the form `a = a` (reflexivity).
   129→
   130→## Syntax
   131→```
   132→rfl
   133→```
   134→
   135→## When to use it
   136→When both sides of an equality are definitionally equal.
   137→-/
   138→TacticDoc rfl
   139→
   140→/-- `constructor` splits a goal with a single constructor (like `∧` or `↔`)
   141→into its components.
   142→
   143→## Syntax
   144→```
   145→constructor
   146→```
   147→
   148→## When to use it
   149→When the goal is `P ∧ Q` (splits into `P` and `Q`) or `P ↔ Q`
   150→(splits into `P → Q` and `Q → P`).
   151→-/
   152→TacticDoc constructor
   153→
   154→/-- `norm_num` evaluates numerical expressions and closes arithmetic
   155→goals that can be decided by computation.
   156→
   157→## Syntax
   158→```
   159→norm_num
   160→```
   161→
   162→## When to use it

import GameServer
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.Basic

/-! ## Documentation for disabled tactics and theorems

These docs are needed by the GameServer when tactics/theorems are disabled.
They are placed here so all level files can reference them.
-/

/-! ### NNG4-baseline tactics

These tactics are assumed known from the Natural Number Game or
equivalent. They are introduced in Level 1 so the game doesn't warn
about missing introductions.
-/

/-- `exact e` closes the goal if `e` has exactly the type of the goal.

## Syntax
```
exact expression
```

## When to use it
When you have a term (variable, function application, constructor)
whose type matches the goal exactly.
-/
TacticDoc exact

/-- `rw [h]` rewrites the goal using an equation or iff `h`.

## Syntax
```
rw [h]           -- rewrite the goal
rw [h] at hyp    -- rewrite a hypothesis
rw [← h]         -- rewrite right-to-left
rw [h1, h2, h3]  -- chain multiple rewrites
```

## When to use it
When you have an equation `h : a = b` or iff `h : P ↔ Q` and want
to replace `a` with `b` (or `P` with `Q`) in the goal or a hypothesis.
-/
TacticDoc rw

/-- `intro x` introduces a universally quantified variable or an
antecedent of an implication into the context.

## Syntax
```
intro x       -- introduce one variable
intro x y z   -- introduce multiple variables
intro h       -- introduce an implication hypothesis
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `∀ x, P x` or `P → Q`.
-/
TacticDoc intro

/-- `cases x with | constructor₁ => ... | constructor₂ => ...`
performs case analysis on `x`, creating one subgoal per constructor.

## Syntax
```
cases x with
| constructor₁ args => tactic₁
| constructor₂ args => tactic₂
```

## When to use it
When you want to consider all possible forms of a value (e.g., natural
numbers are `zero` or `succ n`, propositions with `∨` have `inl` and `inr`).
-/
TacticDoc cases

/-- `have h := expr` introduces a new hypothesis `h` with value `expr`.

## Syntax
```
have h := expr           -- Lean infers the type
have h : type := expr    -- explicit type annotation
have h : type := by ...  -- prove the hypothesis with tactics
```

## When to use it
When you need an intermediate fact that isn't directly in your context.
-/
TacticDoc «have»

/-- `use expr` provides a witness for an existential goal `∃ x, P x`.

## Syntax
```
use expr
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `∃ x, P x` and you know which `x` works.
After `use expr`, the goal becomes `P expr`.
-/
TacticDoc use

/-- `left` selects the left disjunct of a goal `P ∨ Q`.

## Syntax
```
left
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `P ∨ Q` and you want to prove `P`.
-/
TacticDoc left

/-- `right` selects the right disjunct of a goal `P ∨ Q`.

## Syntax
```
right
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `P ∨ Q` and you want to prove `Q`.
-/
TacticDoc right

/-- `rfl` closes a goal of the form `a = a` (reflexivity).

## Syntax
```
rfl
```

## When to use it
When both sides of an equality are definitionally equal.
-/
TacticDoc rfl

/-- `constructor` splits a goal with a single constructor (like `∧` or `↔`)
into its components.

## Syntax
```
constructor
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `P ∧ Q` (splits into `P` and `Q`) or `P ↔ Q`
(splits into `P → Q` and `Q → P`).
-/
TacticDoc constructor

/-- `norm_num` evaluates numerical expressions and closes arithmetic
goals that can be decided by computation.

## Syntax
```
norm_num
```

## When to use it
When the goal is a concrete numerical fact like `2 + 3 = 5` or `7 < 10`.

Disabled in levels where manual arithmetic reasoning is the lesson.
-/
TacticDoc norm_num

/-! ### Baseline disabled tactics

These tactics are disabled globally because they close goals that
the player should learn to handle manually.
-/

/-- `trivial` attempts to close the goal using a combination of
basic tactics (`rfl`, `exact True.intro`, `assumption`).

Disabled because it bypasses the learning of specific proof steps.
-/
TacticDoc trivial

/-- `decide` closes goals that are decidable propositions by
evaluating them computationally.

Disabled because it bypasses manual reasoning about finite types.
-/
TacticDoc «decide»

/-- `native_decide` is like `decide` but uses native code evaluation
for faster computation.

Disabled for the same reason as `decide`.
-/
TacticDoc native_decide

/-- `simp` applies a set of simplification lemmas to the goal
and hypotheses.

Disabled in early levels to force manual rewriting and to ensure
the player understands each proof step.
-/
TacticDoc simp

/-- `aesop` is a general-purpose automation tactic that combines
multiple strategies to close goals.

Disabled to force manual proof construction.
-/
TacticDoc aesop

/-- `simp_all` applies `simp` to all hypotheses and the goal
simultaneously.

Disabled for the same reason as `simp`.
-/
TacticDoc simp_all

/-! ### MeetFin-specific disabled tactics and theorems -/

/-- `fin_cases` automatically performs case analysis on elements of
finite types like `Fin n`. It replaces a variable `x : Fin n` with
each possible value `0, 1, ..., n-1`.

Disabled in early levels to force manual case analysis.
-/
TacticDoc fin_cases

/-- `interval_cases` performs case analysis on a natural number or
integer variable whose value is bounded by hypotheses.

Disabled in early levels to force manual case analysis.
-/
TacticDoc interval_cases

/-- `Fin.forall_fin_two` states that a predicate holds for all
elements of `Fin 2` if and only if it holds for `0` and `1`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.forall_fin_two as "Fin.forall_fin_two" in "Fin"

/-- `Unique.eq_default` states that in a type with exactly one element,
every element equals the default.
-/
TheoremDoc Unique.eq_default as "Unique.eq_default" in "Fin"

/-- `Subsingleton.elim` states that in a type with at most one element,
any two elements are equal.
-/
TheoremDoc Subsingleton.elim as "Subsingleton.elim" in "Fin"

/-! ### FinNavigation-specific disabled theorems -/

/-- `Fin.castSucc_lt_succ` states that `i.castSucc < i.succ` for
any `i : Fin n`. This follows from the value-level fact
`i.val < i.val + 1`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.castSucc_lt_succ as "Fin.castSucc_lt_succ" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.succ_pos` states that `0 < a.succ` for any `a : Fin n`.
This follows from the value-level fact `0 < a.val + 1`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.succ_pos as "Fin.succ_pos" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.succ_inj` states that `a.succ = b.succ ↔ a = b` for
elements of `Fin n`. In other words, `Fin.succ` is injective.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.succ_inj as "Fin.succ_inj" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.castSucc_inj` states that `a.castSucc = b.castSucc ↔ a = b`
for elements of `Fin n`. In other words, `Fin.castSucc` is injective.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.castSucc_inj as "Fin.castSucc_inj" in "Fin"

#### W03: FinTuples (Lecture)

**Type**: Lecture

**Promise**: By the end of this world, the learner will understand that `Fin n -> α` is a "tuple" (ordered list of fixed length), and will be able to construct and decompose tuples using `Fin.cons` and `Fin.snoc`.

**Theorem families**:
- `Fin.cons` — prepend an element to a tuple
- `Fin.snoc` — append an element to a tuple
- `Fin.tail` — drop the first element
- `Fin.cons_zero`, `Fin.cons_succ`
- `Fin.snoc_last`, `Fin.snoc_castSucc`
- Function extensionality for `Fin n -> α`

**Proof-move goals**:
- Build a function `Fin n -> α` by specifying each value
- Use `Fin.cons` to build tuples recursively
- Decompose a tuple using `Fin.tail`
- Prove two tuples are equal by proving they agree at every index (function extensionality)

**Inventory changes**:
- `NewDefinition Fin.cons Fin.snoc Fin.tail`
- `NewTheorem Fin.cons_zero Fin.cons_succ Fin.snoc_last Fin.snoc_castSucc`
- `NewTactic funext` — function extensionality (new tactic)

**Level sketch**:
- L01: `Fin n -> α` IS a tuple. Define a function `Fin 3 -> ℕ` by cases. Lesson: functions from Fin as data.
- L02: `Fin.cons` — build a tuple by prepending. Lesson: recursive tuple construction.
- L03: `Fin.cons_zero` and `Fin.cons_succ` — access elements of a `cons`-built tuple. Lesson: destructor lemmas.
- L04: `Fin.snoc` — build a tuple by appending. Lesson: alternative construction.
- L05: `Fin.tail` — drop the first element. Lesson: tuple decomposition.
- L06: Function extensionality (`funext`). Prove two tuples are equal by proving pointwise equality. Lesson: ext for functions.
- L07 (Boss): Build a tuple, decompose it, and prove an equality. Requires `cons`, access lemmas, and `funext`.

**Dependencies**: FinNavigation

**Pset partner**: PsetFin (W04)

---

#### W04: PsetFin (Problem Set)

**Type**: Pset

**Promise**: The learner retrieves and transfers Fin skills on fresh problems without scaffolding.

**Proof-move goals**:
- Unsupported retrieval of element construction, navigation, and tuple manipulation
- Transfer to fresh surface forms not seen in W01-W03

**Inventory changes**: None new. All W01-W03 inventory available.

**Level sketch**:
- L01: Construct a specific Fin element satisfying a property (retrieval of W01).
- L02: Navigate Fin using succ/castSucc in a new context (retrieval of W02).
- L03: Build a tuple with specified properties (retrieval of W03).
- L04: Prove two Fin elements are equal using ext in a non-obvious setting (transfer).
- L05: Case analysis on Fin combined with tuple access (integration of W01-W03).
- L06 (Boss): Multi-step problem requiring element construction, navigation, tuple building, and extensionality. 5+ integrated moves.

Now I have a clear picture of all the issues. Let me plan the comprehensive fix:

## Summary of required fixes

### P1 defects (MUST fix all):
1. **P1-1**: Fin.snoc omitted entirely - need 2-3 new levels for snoc, snoc_last, snoc_castSucc
2. **P1-2**: Fin.cons_zero / Fin.cons_succ not taught as named lemmas - need a new level with rw
3. **P1-3**: No DisabledTheorem anywhere - add DisabledTheorem for funext and other exploits


4. **P1-4**: Four consecutive rfl-only levels (L02-L05) - restructure for variety
5. **P1-5**: L01 visible hint gives complete solution

### P2 defects (fix if possible):
1. **P2-1**: L08 hints are code-first, not strategy-first
2. **P2-2**: L07 bypassable with exact funext h
3. **P2-3**: World intro mentions ext from MeetFin but L07 reintroduces without acknowledging
4. **P2-4**: No level exercises Fin.cons in non-definitional context

### Red flags:
1. **RF-1**: L01 introduces 2+ new burdens simultaneously
2. **RF-2**: Boss doesn't genuinely integrate

### Enrichment suggestions to implement:
1. Add Fin.snoc levels (2-3 new levels)
2. Add Fin.cons_zero / Fin.cons_succ as explicit NewTheorem level
3. Add empty tuple level (Fin 0 -> alpha uniqueness)
4. Make cons/tail <-> castSucc/last connection explicit

### Playtest fixes required:
1. Redesign boss for genuine integration
2. Split L01 into two levels
3. Add Fin.cons_zero / Fin.cons_succ as NewTheorem
4. Fix L01 hint layering
5. Add DisabledTheorem for funext
6. Break up the consecutive rfl-only stretch in L02-L05
7. Reframe L08 hints to emphasize strategy before tactics

Now I'm mapping out the revised level progression. The current 10-level structure has L01 overloaded with too many concepts, and L02-L05 are monotonous with just rfl. I need to redistribute the content and add the missing Fin operations to create a more engaging arc. The new structure should introduce Fin.cons_zero and Fin.cons_succ earlier as their own theorem, break up the rfl repetition, and ensure the boss actually exercises the full toolkit rather than just being a capstone. Given Tuple** — Given `v = ![10, 20, 30]`, prove `v 1 = 20`. Just `rfl`. Introduces "Fin n → α = tuple" with zero new syntax. (Fixes RF-1 split)

**L02: Vec Notation** — Build a tuple using `![...]`. The old L01's match syntax goes away; instead, show that `![10, 20, 30]` IS the function. Prove something like `![a, b, c] 0 = a`. Still rfl but introduces the notation. (Absorbs old L02+L03 merge)

**L03: Building Functions by Cases** — Introduce match syntax `fun | 0 => ... | 1 => ...`. Now it's the ONLY new burden. (Fixes RF-1 — this was the second burden from old L01)

**L04: Prepending with Fin.cons** — Prove `![a, b, c] = Fin.cons a ![b, c]`. Still rfl. (Same as old L04)

**L05: Accessing Cons Elements** — I need to think through how `Fin.cons_zero` and `Fin.cons_succ` work. The first one, `Fin.cons_zero : Fin.cons a f 0 = a`, is definitional and proved by rfl in Mathlib. The second, `Fin.cons_succ`, handles the successor case but gets cut off here — I'm trying to figure out whether these should be NewTheorems for non-definitional contexts or if there's a better way to structure this lesson.

Actually, a cleaner approach: design a level where the learner must use `rw [Fin.cons_zero]` because the goal isn't immediately reducible. For instance, given a hypothesis like `h : Fin.cons a f = ![1, 2, 3]`, prove `a = 1` by applying `congrFun` to extract the value at index 0, then rewrite with the cons lemma.

But I should verify what `Fin.cons_zero` actually is in Mathlib first—whether it's marked `@[simp]` and whether it reduces definitionally. The plan mentions introducing both `Fin.cons_zero` and `Fin.cons_succ` as new theorems for accessing tuple elements, and the playtest notes suggest adding a level that explicitly uses `rw [Fin.cons_zero]`.

The key insight is that if `Fin.cons a f 0` reduces to `a` by definition, then `rfl` would always work, so I need to construct a goal where rewriting is necessary instead—something like proving `Fin.cons a f 0 = 5` given `a = 5`, where the variable prevents reflexivity from closing the goal directly. I'm realizing the key insight is that while `rfl` works directly on `Fin.cons a f 0`, the named lemma `Fin.cons_zero` becomes essential when the constructor is hidden behind an abstract variable, since then rewriting with the lemma name lets you make progress in contexts where reflexivity alone won't. Let me check what Mathlib actually calls this lemma and what its exact signature is.

I'm thinking through a better pedagogical approach: rather than just showing the lemma in isolation, I should design a level where the goal naturally requires knowing these two key properties—that `Fin.cons a f 0 = a` and `Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i`—so the learner sees why the named lemmas matter for rewriting. The challenge is keeping it simple enough to be instructive without getting bogged down in complexity.

The real issue is that these lemmas have never been explicitly taught as named theorems, and `Fin.cons` has only appeared in definitional contexts where reflexivity works. So I need two levels: one that introduces `Fin.cons_zero` and `Fin.cons_succ` as `NewTheorem` entries so they're available in the learner's toolkit, and then another that has them apply these lemmas via rewriting in a concrete example where they're given an equality and need to simplify a function application. rewrite it to `a`, making the goal `a = a` which `rfl` closes automatically. So `rw [h, Fin.cons_zero]` works as a complete proof. But actually, `rfl` alone suffices after `rw [h]` since `Fin.cons a f 0` reduces definitionally to `a`, meaning these lemmas get bypassed in concrete cases. The real utility emerges when `n` is a variable rather than a concrete number—that's when you actually need these lemmas instead of relying on definitional equality.

Looking at how `Fin.cons` is actually defined through pattern matching, when the input is `0` it immediately reduces to `a`, so `rfl` should work regardless. This means `Fin.cons a f 0` always reduces to `a` by definition, making the named lemma somewhat redundant in practice.

The real use case for `rw [Fin.cons_zero]` emerges when you're working with `Fin.cons a f 0` as part of a larger expression—like in an arithmetic operation or comparison—where `rfl` can't simplify the whole goal, but rewriting that specific subexpression lets you make progress. The key insight is that the pedagogical value lies in introducing the lemma name itself—even if the level is straightforward, having `Fin.cons_zero` in the learner's toolkit means they'll recognize when to apply it in more complex scenarios later. I'm wondering whether `Fin.cons_zero` might fail to reduce definitionally in edge cases, particularly when the index `0` is constructed in different ways, since the cons operation pattern-matches on whether the underlying natural number is zero or a successor.

My plan is to create two levels: first, one that introduces `cons_zero` and `cons_succ` as named theorems with reflexivity proofs, then a follow-up level where the learner must explicitly rewrite using these theorems in a context where reflexivity alone won't suffice—something like simplifying an equality that involves the cons operation.

Building the example level... I'm verifying that these lemma names actually exist in Mathlib, then I'll move on to designing the `Fin.snoc` levels, which work similarly but append to the back of the function instead.

content.thinking:
All the lemma names exist and have the right types. Now I notice that `Fin.cons`, `Fin.cons_zero`, `Fin.cons_succ`, `Fin.snoc` etc. are all dependent — they work with `α : Fin (n + 1) → Sort u_1` (a dependent type family), not just `α : Sort*`. For our purposes, we'll use the non-dependent case where `α` is constant.

Also, `Fin.snoc` has type:
```
{n : ℕ} → {α : Fin (n + 1) → Sort u_1} → ((i : Fin n) → α i.castSucc) → α (Fin.last n) → (i : Fin (n + 1)) → α i


```

In the non-dependent case, this simplifies to `(Fin n → α) → α → Fin (n + 1) → α`. The key thing to notice is the argument order: `Fin.snoc p x` takes the tuple first, then the element, which is opposite to `Fin.cons x p`. The lemmas `snoc_last` and `snoc_castSucc` tell us that `snoc` returns the new element when applied to the last position, and returns the original tuple elements when applied to earlier positions.

But there's something odd about the theorem signatures—they seem to flip the argument order compared to the function itself, taking `x` before `p` in the type signature even though `Fin.snoc` applies them the other way around.
```
Fin.snoc ![a, b] c = ![a, b, c]
```

Let me verify this works by checking what happens at each index: at position 0 and 1, I should get `a` and `b` respectively from the original vector, and at the final position, I should get `c`.

The issue is that `Fin.snoc` uses `Fin.lastCases` for case analysis while `![a, b, c]` uses `Fin.cons`, so they have different definitional structures and won't reduce to the same thing by reflexivity alone.

Let me test this with a quick example to verify.

content.thinking:
The issue is that `Fin.cons`, `Fin.snoc`, etc. have DEPENDENT types in Lean 4 / Mathlib. The dependent type parameter `α : Fin (n + 1) → Sort u_1` is causing problems. When we write `Fin.cons a f` where `a : ℕ` and `f : Fin 2 → ℕ`, the type of `Fin.cons` in the non-dependent case is inferred as `α = fun _ => ℕ`. But `Fin.cons_zero a f` tries to construct a proof of type `Fin.cons a f 0 = a` in the DEPENDENT setting, and the result type is `α 0 = α 0` which is `(fun _ => ℕ) 0 = ...` etc.


The error reveals that `Fin.cons a f 0` has type `α 0` rather than `ℕ`, so even though `α` is the constant function `fun _ => ℕ`, Lean isn't automatically reducing `α 0` to `ℕ` when I try to apply `Fin.cons_zero a f` directly. I need to find a way to make this unification work or use a different approach to handle the dependent type mismatch. the `show` tactic to help, or using `@` to provide explicit type arguments. Or maybe I should use the non-dependent versions.

Actually, looking more carefully: in Lean 4, `Fin.cons` is defined with a dependent type, so when I apply it with `a : ℕ` and `f : Fin n → ℕ`, the type parameter `α` should unify to the constant function `fun _ => ℕ`. The issue with `Fin.cons_zero a f` might be that Lean is struggling with this dependent type unification, so I should try providing explicit type arguments like `@Fin.cons_zero ℕ _ _ a f` to help it along. I'm trying different ways to explicitly specify the dependent type to help Lean infer that the function type is `fun _ => ℕ`. I could use the `@` operator to pass all arguments explicitly, or add a `show` tactic to clarify the expected type before applying the lemma. Since `rfl` works definitionally for this equality, the real goal is just demonstrating the lemma name itself. I'm wondering if there's a simpler approach here—maybe `Matrix.cons_val_zero` exists as a lemma, or I should check whether `Matrix.vecCons` is actually just `Fin.cons` under the hood. The `![...]` notation seems to use `Matrix.vecCons`, so I need to verify if that's the same thing and what lemmas are available for it. import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

-- Test @ explicit snoc with non-dep type

    @Fin.snoc 2 (fun _ => ℕ) f c (Fin.last 2) = c := by
  exact @Fin.snoc_last 2 (fun _ => ℕ) c f

example (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (c : ℕ) (i : Fin 2) :
    @Fin.snoc 2 (fun _ => ℕ) f c i.castSucc = f i := by
  exact @Fin.snoc_castSucc 2 (fun _ => ℕ) c f i

-- Can we make it nicer? Use show or type ascription?
example (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (c : ℕ) :
    @Fin.snoc 2 (fun _ => ℕ) f c = (![f 0, f 1, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) := by
  ext ⟨v, hv⟩
  cases v with
  | zero => rfl
  | succ n =>
    cases n with
    | zero => rfl
    | succ m =>
      cases m with
      | zero => rfl
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

-- Hmm, let me try a totally different approach.
-- Maybe use a helper definition for non-dep snoc
abbrev vecSnoc {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (f : Fin n → α) (x : α) : Fin (n + 1) → α :=
  @Fin.snoc n (fun _ => α) f x

-- Then use it
example (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (c : ℕ) : vecSnoc f c (Fin.last 2) = c := by
  exact @Fin.snoc_last 2 (fun _ => ℕ) c f

  @Fin.snoc 2 (fun _ => ℕ) f c

-- How about: define as a wrapper
def Fin.snoc' {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (f : Fin n → α) (x : α) : Fin (n + 1) → α :=
  @Fin.snoc n (fun _ => α) f x

theorem Fin.snoc'_last {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (f : Fin n → α) (x : α) :
    Fin.snoc' f x (Fin.last n) = x :=
  @Fin.snoc_last n (fun _ => α) x f

theorem Fin.snoc'_castSucc {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (f : Fin n → α) (x : α) (i : Fin n) :
    Fin.snoc' f x i.castSucc = f i :=
  @Fin.snoc_castSucc n (fun _ => α) x f i

-- Test the wrappers
example (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (c : ℕ) : Fin.snoc' f c (Fin.last 2) = c := by
  exact Fin.snoc'_last f c

example (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (c : ℕ) (i : Fin 2) : Fin.snoc' f c i.castSucc = f i := by
  exact Fin.snoc'_castSucc f c i

cat > /tmp/check_proofs8.lean << 'LEANEOF'
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

-- Define non-dependent wrappers
theorem cons_val_zero' {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (a : α) (f : Fin n → α) :
    (Fin.cons a f : Fin (n+1) → α) 0 = a := rfl

theorem cons_val_succ' {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (a : α) (f : Fin n → α) (i : Fin n) :
    (Fin.cons a f : Fin (n+1) → α) i.succ = f i := rfl

-- Test usage
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) : (Fin.cons a f : Fin 3 → ℕ) 0 = a := by
  exact cons_val_zero' a f

example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (i : Fin 2) : (Fin.cons a f : Fin 3 → ℕ) i.succ = f i := by
  exact cons_val_succ' a f i

-- Now define non-dependent snoc wrapper
def vecSnoc {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (f : Fin n → α) (x : α) : Fin (n + 1) → α :=
  @Fin.snoc n (fun _ => α) f x

theorem vecSnoc_last {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (f : Fin n → α) (x : α) :
    vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x :=
  @Fin.snoc_last n (fun _ => α) x f

theorem vecSnoc_castSucc {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (f : Fin n → α) (x : α) (i : Fin n) :
    vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i :=
  @Fin.snoc_castSucc n (fun _ => α) x f i

-- Test: does vecSnoc reduce at concrete indices?
example (a b c : ℕ) : vecSnoc ![a, b] c (Fin.last 2) = c := by
  exact vecSnoc_last ![a, b] c

example (a b c : ℕ) : vecSnoc ![a, b] c (Fin.castSucc 0) = a := by
  exact vecSnoc_castSucc ![a, b] c 0

-- Test: vecSnoc = vec notation
example (a b c : ℕ) : vecSnoc ![a, b] c = (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) := by
  ext ⟨v, hv⟩
  cases v with
  | zero => rfl
  | succ n =>
    cases n with
    | zero => rfl
    | succ m =>
      cases m with
      | zero => rfl
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

-- Test: Does rfl work for concrete vecSnoc?
example : vecSnoc (![1, 2] : Fin 2 → ℕ) 3 (Fin.last 2) = 3 := by rfl
example : vecSnoc (![1, 2] : Fin 2 → ℕ) 3 (Fin.castSucc 0) = 1 := by rfl

-- Test boss proof
example (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h0 : f 0 = 1) (ht : Fin.tail f = ![2, 3]) :
    f = ![1, 2, 3] := by
  have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm
  rw [h0, ht] at key
  exact key

-- Another important test: does `Fin.cons` display as `Fin.cons` or `Matrix.vecCons`?
-- Can the learner write `Fin.cons` in proofs?
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) : (![a, f 0, f 1] : Fin 3 → ℕ) = Fin.cons a f := by
  ext ⟨v, hv⟩
  cases v with
  | zero => rfl
  | succ n =>
    cases n with
    | zero => rfl
    | succ m =>
      cases m with
      | zero => rfl
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

-- Can we prove this by rfl?
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) : (Fin.cons a f : Fin 3 → ℕ) = ![a, f 0, f 1] := by
  rfl

cat > /tmp/check_final.lean << 'LEANEOF'
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

-- Define our own cons access lemmas matching Fin.cons syntax
theorem Fin.cons_val_zero {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (a : α) (f : Fin n → α) :
    (Fin.cons a f : Fin (n+1) → α) 0 = a := rfl

theorem Fin.cons_val_succ {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (a : α) (f : Fin n → α) (i : Fin n) :
    (Fin.cons a f : Fin (n+1) → α) i.succ = f i := rfl

-- Define non-dep snoc
def vecSnoc {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (f : Fin n → α) (x : α) : Fin (n + 1) → α :=
  @Fin.snoc n (fun _ => α) f x

theorem vecSnoc_last {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (f : Fin n → α) (x : α) :
    vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x :=
  @Fin.snoc_last n (fun _ => α) x f

theorem vecSnoc_castSucc {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (f : Fin n → α) (x : α) (i : Fin n) :
    vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i :=
  @Fin.snoc_castSucc n (fun _ => α) x f i

-- Test: rw works with our cons_val_zero
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (h : a = 5) : (Fin.cons a f : Fin 3 → ℕ) 0 = 5 := by
  rw [Fin.cons_val_zero]
  exact h

-- Test: exact works
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) : (Fin.cons a f : Fin 3 → ℕ) 0 = a := by
  exact Fin.cons_val_zero a f

-- Test: rw with cons_val_succ
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (h : f 0 = 7) : (Fin.cons a f : Fin 3 → ℕ) 1 = 7 := by
  rw [show (1 : Fin 3) = (0 : Fin 2).succ from rfl, Fin.cons_val_succ]
  exact h

-- Hmm that's ugly. Let's try direct approach
example (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (h : f 0 = 7) : (Fin.cons a f : Fin 3 → ℕ) 1 = 7 := by
  -- (Fin.cons a f) 1 = f 0 = 7
  exact h  -- Does this work since Fin.cons a f 1 reduces to f 0?

-- Test vecSnoc
example (a b c : ℕ) : vecSnoc ![a, b] c (Fin.last 2) = c := by
  exact vecSnoc_last ![a, b] c

example (a b c : ℕ) (i : Fin 2) : vecSnoc ![a, b] c i.castSucc = ![a, b] i := by
  exact vecSnoc_castSucc ![a, b] c i

-- Test vecSnoc = ![...]
example (a b c : ℕ) : vecSnoc ![a, b] c = (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) := by
  ext ⟨v, hv⟩
  cases v with
  | zero => rfl
  | succ n =>
    cases n with
    | zero => rfl
    | succ m =>
      cases m with
      | zero => rfl
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

-- Test: rw with vecSnoc_last
example (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (c : ℕ) (h : c = 10) : vecSnoc f c (Fin.last 2) = 10 := by
  rw [vecSnoc_last]
  exact h

-- Test: rw with vecSnoc_castSucc
example (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) (c : ℕ) (h : f 0 = 10) : vecSnoc f c (Fin.castSucc 0) = 10 := by
  rw [vecSnoc_castSucc]
  exact h

-- Test: empty tuple uniqueness
example (f g : Fin 0 → ℕ) : f = g := by
  ext i
  exact Fin.elim0 i

-- Test: boss proof
example (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h0 : f 0 = 1) (ht : Fin.tail f = ![2, 3]) :
    f = ![1, 2, 3] := by
  have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm
  rw [h0, ht] at key
  exact key

-- Test: does Fin.cons a f display correctly in goal?
-- Can learner write 'exact Fin.cons_self_tail f'?
example (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) : (Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) : Fin 3 → ℕ) = f := by
  exact Fin.cons_self_tail f

-- Test: funext bypass check
example (f g : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h : ∀ i, f i = g i) : f = g := by
  exact funext h
LEANEOF

import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

-- Non-dep cons access lemmas
theorem Fin.cons_val_zero' {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (a : α) (f : Fin n → α) :
    (Fin.cons a f : Fin (n+1) → α) 0 = a := rfl

theorem Fin.cons_val_succ' {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (a : α) (f : Fin n → α) (i : Fin n) :
    (Fin.cons a f : Fin (n+1) → α) i.succ = f i := rfl

-- Boss candidate
example (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h0 : f 0 = 1) (h1 : f 1 = 2) (h2 : f 2 = 3) :
    (Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) : Fin 3 → ℕ) = ![1, 2, 3] := by
  rw [Fin.cons_self_tail]
  ext ⟨v, hv⟩
  cases v with
  | zero => exact h0
  | succ n =>
    cases n with
    | zero => exact h1
    | succ m =>
      cases m with
      | zero => exact h2
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

-- Alt boss path (ext directly, no rw cons_self_tail)
example (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h0 : f 0 = 1) (h1 : f 1 = 2) (h2 : f 2 = 3) :
    (Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) : Fin 3 → ℕ) = ![1, 2, 3] := by
  ext ⟨v, hv⟩
  cases v with
  | zero => exact h0
  | succ n =>
    cases n with
    | zero => exact h1
    | succ m =>
      cases m with
      | zero => exact h2
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

-- Test 'use' with match syntax
example : ∃ f : Fin 3 → ℕ, f 0 = 10 ∧ f 1 = 20 ∧ f 2 = 30 := by
  use fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30

-- Boss with have + rw approach
example (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h0 : f 0 = 1) (h1 : f 1 = 2) (h2 : f 2 = 3) :
    (Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) : Fin 3 → ℕ) = ![1, 2, 3] := by
  rw [Fin.cons_self_tail, show f = ![1, 2, 3] from ?_]
  ext ⟨v, hv⟩
  cases v with
  | zero => exact h0
  | succ n =>
    cases n with
    | zero => exact h1
    | succ m =>
      cases m with
      | zero => exact h2
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

-- Simplest integration boss: h0 + ht approach
example (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h0 : f 0 = 1) (ht : Fin.tail f = ![2, 3]) :
    (Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) : Fin 3 → ℕ) = ![1, 2, 3] := by
  rw [h0, ht]

-- Even simpler - just rw!
-- That's only 2 moves. Too simple for a boss.

-- Let's try the form where cons_self_tail is needed:
example (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h0 : f 0 = 1) (ht : Fin.tail f = ![2, 3]) :
    f = ![1, 2, 3] := by
  have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm
  rw [h0, ht] at key
  exact key

The LHS is `f ⟨n+1, hv⟩`. By definition of `Fin.tail`, this equals `Fin.tail f ⟨n, ...⟩`. And by `ht`, `Fin.tail f = ![2, 3]`. So `Fin.tail f ⟨n, ...⟩ = ![2, 3] ⟨n, ...⟩`. The RHS is `![1, 2, 3] ⟨n+1, hv⟩`, which also unfolds to `![2, 3] ⟨n, ...⟩`. Both sides are equal, though the proof terms might differ slightly due to different bound proofs, so I'd need to apply `congr_fun` to the inductive hypothesis to get the exact equality with matching proofs. So the alternative approach would be to explicitly construct the key equality by rewriting with the inductive hypothesis, but that doesn't actually simplify things—the rewrite wouldn't directly apply in the goal context the way I'd need it to. Actually, there's a cleaner path: breaking down the successor case with nested pattern matching on `n`, starting with the zero case where I can apply `congr_fun` to extract the specific value from the tail equality, then handling the remaining cases similarly.

This approach is straightforward and avoids the type complications. I'll include `congr_fun` in the hints since it's essential here. Rather than overthinking this further, I'm going to commit to the solution—the hints will guide toward the `have` and `rw` strategy as the main path, and I'll document the nested cases approach as an alternative in the conclusion.

/-- `Subsingleton.elim` states that in a type with at most one element,
any two elements are equal.
-/
TheoremDoc Subsingleton.elim as "Subsingleton.elim" in "Fin"

/-! ### FinNavigation-specific disabled theorems -/

/-- `Fin.castSucc_lt_succ` states that `i.castSucc < i.succ` for
any `i : Fin n`. This follows from the value-level fact
`i.val < i.val + 1`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.castSucc_lt_succ as "Fin.castSucc_lt_succ" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.succ_pos` states that `0 < a.succ` for any `a : Fin n`.
This follows from the value-level fact `0 < a.val + 1`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.succ_pos as "Fin.succ_pos" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.succ_inj` states that `a.succ = b.succ ↔ a = b` for
elements of `Fin n`. In other words, `Fin.succ` is injective.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.succ_inj as "Fin.succ_inj" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.castSucc_inj` states that `a.castSucc = b.castSucc ↔ a = b`
for elements of `Fin n`. In other words, `Fin.castSucc` is injective.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.castSucc_inj as "Fin.castSucc_inj" in "Fin"

input.new_string:
TheoremDoc Fin.castSucc_inj as "Fin.castSucc_inj" in "Fin"

/-! ### FinTuples-specific definitions and theorems -/

/-- Non-dependent version of `Fin.cons_zero`.
`Fin.cons a f 0 = a` — accessing the head of a cons'd tuple returns the prepended element. -/
theorem Fin.cons_val_zero {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (a : α) (f : Fin n → α) :
    (Fin.cons a f : Fin (n+1) → α) 0 = a := rfl

/-- Non-dependent version of `Fin.cons_succ`.
`Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i` — accessing later elements of a cons'd tuple returns the tail. -/
theorem Fin.cons_val_succ {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (a : α) (f : Fin n → α) (i : Fin n) :
    (Fin.cons a f : Fin (n+1) → α) i.succ = f i := rfl

/-- `vecSnoc f x` appends element `x` to the end of tuple `f`,
producing a tuple of length `n + 1`.

This is the non-dependent wrapper around `Fin.snoc` for use with
concrete types like `Fin n → ℕ`.

## Type
`vecSnoc : (Fin n → α) → α → Fin (n + 1) → α`

## Element access
- `vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x` (the appended element)
- `vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i` (the original tuple)

## Example
`vecSnoc ![10, 20] 30 = ![10, 20, 30]`
-/
def vecSnoc {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (f : Fin n → α) (x : α) : Fin (n + 1) → α :=
  @Fin.snoc n (fun _ => α) f x

/-- Accessing the last element of a snoc'd tuple gives back the appended element. -/
theorem vecSnoc_last {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (f : Fin n → α) (x : α) :
    vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x :=
  @Fin.snoc_last n (fun _ => α) x f

/-- Accessing an earlier element of a snoc'd tuple gives the original tuple's value. -/
theorem vecSnoc_castSucc {n : ℕ} {α : Type*} (f : Fin n → α) (x : α) (i : Fin n) :
    vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i :=
  @Fin.snoc_castSucc n (fun _ => α) x f i

/-- `funext` is the function extensionality theorem: if two functions
agree on every input, they are equal.

Disabled in levels where the `ext` tactic should be used interactively.
-/
TheoremDoc funext as "funext" in "Fin"

import GameServer
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.Basic

/-! ## Documentation for disabled tactics and theorems

These docs are needed by the GameServer when tactics/theorems are disabled.
They are placed here so all level files can reference them.
-/

/-! ### NNG4-baseline tactics

These tactics are assumed known from the Natural Number Game or
equivalent. They are introduced in Level 1 so the game doesn't warn
about missing introductions.
-/

/-- `exact e` closes the goal if `e` has exactly the type of the goal.

## Syntax
```
exact expression
```

## When to use it
When you have a term (variable, function application, constructor)
whose type matches the goal exactly.
-/
TacticDoc exact

/-- `rw [h]` rewrites the goal using an equation or iff `h`.

## Syntax
```
rw [h]           -- rewrite the goal
rw [h] at hyp    -- rewrite a hypothesis
rw [← h]         -- rewrite right-to-left
rw [h1, h2, h3]  -- chain multiple rewrites
```

## When to use it
When you have an equation `h : a = b` or iff `h : P ↔ Q` and want
to replace `a` with `b` (or `P` with `Q`) in the goal or a hypothesis.
-/
TacticDoc rw

/-- `intro x` introduces a universally quantified variable or an
antecedent of an implication into the context.

## Syntax
```
intro x       -- introduce one variable
intro x y z   -- introduce multiple variables
intro h       -- introduce an implication hypothesis
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `∀ x, P x` or `P → Q`.
-/
TacticDoc intro

/-- `cases x with | constructor₁ => ... | constructor₂ => ...`
performs case analysis on `x`, creating one subgoal per constructor.

## Syntax
```
cases x with
| constructor₁ args => tactic₁
| constructor₂ args => tactic₂
```

## When to use it
When you want to consider all possible forms of a value (e.g., natural
numbers are `zero` or `succ n`, propositions with `∨` have `inl` and `inr`).
-/
TacticDoc cases

/-- `have h := expr` introduces a new hypothesis `h` with value `expr`.

## Syntax
```
have h := expr           -- Lean infers the type
have h : type := expr    -- explicit type annotation
have h : type := by ...  -- prove the hypothesis with tactics
```

## When to use it
When you need an intermediate fact that isn't directly in your context.
-/
TacticDoc «have»

/-- `use expr` provides a witness for an existential goal `∃ x, P x`.

## Syntax
```
use expr
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `∃ x, P x` and you know which `x` works.
After `use expr`, the goal becomes `P expr`.
-/
TacticDoc use

/-- `left` selects the left disjunct of a goal `P ∨ Q`.

## Syntax
```
left
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `P ∨ Q` and you want to prove `P`.
-/
TacticDoc left

/-- `right` selects the right disjunct of a goal `P ∨ Q`.

## Syntax
```
right
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `P ∨ Q` and you want to prove `Q`.
-/
TacticDoc right

/-- `rfl` closes a goal of the form `a = a` (reflexivity).

## Syntax
```
rfl
```

## When to use it
When both sides of an equality are definitionally equal.
-/
TacticDoc rfl

/-- `constructor` splits a goal with a single constructor (like `∧` or `↔`)
into its components.

## Syntax
```
constructor
```

## When to use it
When the goal is `P ∧ Q` (splits into `P` and `Q`) or `P ↔ Q`
(splits into `P → Q` and `Q → P`).
-/
TacticDoc constructor

/-- `norm_num` evaluates numerical expressions and closes arithmetic
goals that can be decided by computation.

## Syntax
```
norm_num
```

## When to use it
When the goal is a concrete numerical fact like `2 + 3 = 5` or `7 < 10`.

Disabled in levels where manual arithmetic reasoning is the lesson.
-/
TacticDoc norm_num

/-! ### Baseline disabled tactics

These tactics are disabled globally because they close goals that
the player should learn to handle manually.
-/

/-- `trivial` attempts to close the goal using a combination of
basic tactics (`rfl`, `exact True.intro`, `assumption`).

Disabled because it bypasses the learning of specific proof steps.
-/
TacticDoc trivial

/-- `decide` closes goals that are decidable propositions by
evaluating them computationally.

Disabled because it bypasses manual reasoning about finite types.
-/
TacticDoc «decide»

/-- `native_decide` is like `decide` but uses native code evaluation
for faster computation.

Disabled for the same reason as `decide`.
-/
TacticDoc native_decide

/-- `simp` applies a set of simplification lemmas to the goal
and hypotheses.

Disabled in early levels to force manual rewriting and to ensure
the player understands each proof step.
-/
TacticDoc simp

/-- `aesop` is a general-purpose automation tactic that combines
multiple strategies to close goals.

Disabled to force manual proof construction.
-/
TacticDoc aesop

/-- `simp_all` applies `simp` to all hypotheses and the goal
simultaneously.

Disabled for the same reason as `simp`.
-/
TacticDoc simp_all

/-! ### MeetFin-specific disabled tactics and theorems -/

/-- `fin_cases` automatically performs case analysis on elements of
finite types like `Fin n`. It replaces a variable `x : Fin n` with
each possible value `0, 1, ..., n-1`.

Disabled in early levels to force manual case analysis.
-/
TacticDoc fin_cases

/-- `interval_cases` performs case analysis on a natural number or
integer variable whose value is bounded by hypotheses.

Disabled in early levels to force manual case analysis.
-/
TacticDoc interval_cases

/-- `Fin.forall_fin_two` states that a predicate holds for all
elements of `Fin 2` if and only if it holds for `0` and `1`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.forall_fin_two as "Fin.forall_fin_two" in "Fin"

/-- `Unique.eq_default` states that in a type with exactly one element,
every element equals the default.
-/
TheoremDoc Unique.eq_default as "Unique.eq_default" in "Fin"

/-- `Subsingleton.elim` states that in a type with at most one element,
any two elements are equal.
-/
TheoremDoc Subsingleton.elim as "Subsingleton.elim" in "Fin"

/-! ### FinNavigation-specific disabled theorems -/

/-- `Fin.castSucc_lt_succ` states that `i.castSucc < i.succ` for
any `i : Fin n`. This follows from the value-level fact
`i.val < i.val + 1`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.castSucc_lt_succ as "Fin.castSucc_lt_succ" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.succ_pos` states that `0 < a.succ` for any `a : Fin n`.
This follows from the value-level fact `0 < a.val + 1`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.succ_pos as "Fin.succ_pos" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.succ_inj` states that `a.succ = b.succ ↔ a = b` for
elements of `Fin n`. In other words, `Fin.succ` is injective.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.succ_inj as "Fin.succ_inj" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.castSucc_inj` states that `a.castSucc = b.castSucc ↔ a = b`
for elements of `Fin n`. In other words, `Fin.castSucc` is injective.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.castSucc_inj as "Fin.castSucc_inj" in "Fin"

input.content:
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 1

Title "Functions as Tuples"

Introduction "
# Functions from Fin as Tuples

In the previous worlds, you learned that `Fin n` is the type of
natural numbers less than `n`. Now comes a key insight:

**A function `Fin n → α` is the same thing as an `n`-tuple** — an
ordered list of `n` elements from `α`.

Why? A function from `Fin 3 → ℕ` is determined by three values:
what it returns at `0`, at `1`, and at `2`. Those three values, in
order, form a triple. Conversely, any triple $(a_0, a_1, a_2)$
defines a function `Fin 3 → ℕ` by $i \\mapsto a_i$.

In Lean, `![10, 20, 30]` creates a function `Fin 3 → ℕ` that
returns `10` at index `0`, `20` at index `1`, and `30` at index `2`.
The `!` prefix distinguishes this from a plain list — it's a
**vector** (a function from `Fin n`), not a linked list.

**Your task**: Verify that the element at index 1 of `![10, 20, 30]`
is `20`. (Remember: indexing starts at 0.)
"

/-- The element at index 1 of `![10, 20, 30]` is 20. -/
Statement : (![10, 20, 30] : Fin 3 → ℕ) 1 = 20 := by
  Hint "The value `![10, 20, 30] 1` reduces to `20` by definition.
  Use `rfl`."
  rfl

Conclusion "
You just accessed an element of a 3-tuple. The key idea:

$$\\texttt{![10, 20, 30]}$$

creates a function `Fin 3 → ℕ`. Evaluating it at index `1` gives
`20` — the second element (counting from 0).

This is analogous to subscript notation in mathematics:
if $v = (10, 20, 30)$, then $v_1 = 20$.
In Lean, `v 1` plays the role of $v_1$.

Since the value is determined by the definition, `rfl` closes
the equation.
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num

import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 2

Title "Vec Notation with Variables"

Introduction "
# Tuples Work with Variables Too

The `![...]` notation doesn't just work with concrete numbers.
You can use variables:

If `a b c : ℕ`, then `![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ` is a 3-tuple
whose values are `a`, `b`, and `c`.

Element access still reduces by definition:
- `![a, b, c] 0 = a`
- `![a, b, c] 1 = b`
- `![a, b, c] 2 = c`

These hold by `rfl` because `![a, b, c]` is *defined* to return
`a` at index 0, `b` at index 1, and `c` at index 2 — regardless
of what `a`, `b`, `c` actually are.

**Your task**: Prove that the first element of `![a, b, c]` is `a`.
"

/-- Accessing the first element of a variable tuple. -/

import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 3

Title "Building Functions by Cases"

Introduction "
# Constructing a Tuple by Pattern Matching

You've seen how to *read* tuples. Now let's *build* one.

To define a function `Fin 3 → ℕ` by listing what it returns
at each input, write:

$$\\texttt{fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30}$$

Each clause `| i => value` says what the function returns at
index `i`. This is called **pattern matching** on the input.

**Your task**: Build a 3-tuple — a function `Fin 3 → ℕ` — whose
values at 0, 1, 2 are 10, 20, 30. The goal asks you to provide
the function AND prove it has the right values.

Package the function with its proofs using the anonymous
constructor: `⟨function, proof1, proof2, proof3⟩`.
"

/-- A function `Fin 3 → ℕ` that maps 0 to 10, 1 to 20, and 2 to 30. -/
Statement : ∃ f : Fin 3 → ℕ, f 0 = 10 ∧ f 1 = 20 ∧ f 2 = 30 := by
  Hint "You need to provide the function AND the proofs.
  The function maps each index to its value using match syntax:
  `fun | 0 => value0 | 1 => value1 | 2 => value2`.

  Package everything with angle brackets:
  `exact ⟨function, proof1, proof2, proof3⟩`."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`exact ⟨fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30, rfl, rfl, rfl⟩`"
  exact ⟨fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30, rfl, rfl, rfl⟩

Conclusion "
You just built a 3-tuple from scratch by defining a function
from `Fin 3`.

The key syntax: `fun | 0 => 10 | 1 => 20 | 2 => 30` defines a
function by listing its value at each input. This is exactly like
writing the tuple $(10, 20, 30)$.

The three `rfl`s verify that the function has the right values.
Since the values are determined by the definition, each equation
holds definitionally.

In the next level, you'll see the machinery under the hood:
`Fin.cons`, which builds tuples by prepending one element
at a time.
"

input.content:
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 4

Title "Prepending with Fin.cons"

Introduction "
# Fin.cons: Building Tuples One Element at a Time

Under the hood, `![a, b, c]` is built using **`Fin.cons`** —
a function that prepends one element to an existing tuple.

`Fin.cons` takes:
- An element `a : α` (the new head)
- A tuple `f : Fin n → α` (the existing tail)

and produces a tuple `Fin.cons a f : Fin (n + 1) → α` that
starts with `a` followed by the elements of `f`.

The notation `![a, b, c]` is shorthand for nested `Fin.cons`:
$$\\texttt{![a, b, c]} = \\texttt{Fin.cons } a \\;\\texttt{![b, c]}$$

This is the tuple analog of how `List.cons` builds lists —
prepend one element at a time.

**Your task**: Prove that `![a, b, c]` equals `Fin.cons a ![b, c]`.
This is true by definition.
"

/-- `![a, b, c]` is the same as `Fin.cons a ![b, c]`. -/
Statement (a b c : ℕ) :
    (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) = Fin.cons a ![b, c] := by
  Hint "This equality holds by definition — `![a, b, c]`
  IS `Fin.cons a ![b, c]`. Use `rfl`."
  rfl

Conclusion "
`Fin.cons` is the fundamental building block for tuples:
- Start with the empty tuple `!![] : Fin 0 → α`
- Prepend `c`: `Fin.cons c !![] = ![c] : Fin 1 → α`
- Prepend `b`: `Fin.cons b ![c] = ![b, c] : Fin 2 → α`
- Prepend `a`: `Fin.cons a ![b, c] = ![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → α`

Each `Fin.cons` increases the tuple length by 1.

This is the `Fin` analog of how $\\texttt{cons}$ builds linked lists.
The difference: a linked list has variable length, while
`Fin n → α` has the length `n` baked into the type.
"

/-- `Fin.cons a f` prepends element `a` to tuple `f`,
producing a tuple of length `n + 1`.

## Syntax
```
Fin.cons a f      -- explicit
![a, b, c]        -- ![a, b, c] = Fin.cons a ![b, c]
```

## Type
`Fin.cons : α → (Fin n → α) → (Fin (n + 1) → α)`

## Element access
- `Fin.cons a f 0 = a` (the new head) — `Fin.cons_val_zero`
- `Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i` (the old tail) — `Fin.cons_val_succ`

## Example
`Fin.cons 10 ![20, 30] = ![10, 20, 30]`
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.cons as "Fin.cons"

NewDefinition Fin.cons

import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 5

Title "Cons Access Lemmas"

Introduction "
# Named Access Lemmas for Fin.cons

You know that `Fin.cons a f 0 = a` and `Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i`
hold by definition (`rfl`). But these facts have official names
that you'll need in later worlds when using `rw`:

- **`Fin.cons_val_zero`**: `Fin.cons a f 0 = a`
  — the head of a cons'd tuple is the prepended element

- **`Fin.cons_val_succ`**: `Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i`
  — the tail elements are the original tuple

These names let you rewrite `Fin.cons` expressions inside
larger contexts where `rfl` alone wouldn't close the goal.

**Your task**: Prove both access lemmas. Use `constructor` to
split the conjunction, then `exact Fin.cons_val_zero a f` for the
first part and `intro i` followed by `exact Fin.cons_val_succ a f i`
for the second.
"

/-- The two access lemmas for Fin.cons. -/
Statement (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) :
    (Fin.cons a f : Fin 3 → ℕ) 0 = a ∧
    ∀ i : Fin 2, (Fin.cons a f : Fin 3 → ℕ) i.succ = f i := by
  Hint "Use `constructor` to split the conjunction into two goals."
  constructor
  · Hint "The goal is `Fin.cons a f 0 = a`.
    Use `exact Fin.cons_val_zero a f`."
    Hint (hidden := true) "Or just `rfl` — this is a definitional equality."
    exact Fin.cons_val_zero a f
  · Hint "The goal is `∀ i, Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i`.
    Start with `intro i` to introduce the index."
    intro i
    Hint "Now use `exact Fin.cons_val_succ a f i`."
    exact Fin.cons_val_succ a f i

Conclusion "
You now have two named access lemmas in your inventory:

| Lemma | Statement |
|---|---|
| `Fin.cons_val_zero` | `Fin.cons a f 0 = a` |
| `Fin.cons_val_succ` | `Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i` |

While `rfl` also works for these (they're definitional), the
named versions are useful when you need to rewrite a `Fin.cons`
expression inside a larger goal — for example,
`rw [Fin.cons_val_zero]` simplifies `Fin.cons a f 0` to `a`
even when the full goal isn't `rfl`-closable.
"

/-- `Fin.cons_val_zero a f` states that `Fin.cons a f 0 = a`.

The head of a cons'd tuple is the prepended element.

## When to use it
When you need to simplify `Fin.cons a f 0` to `a` using `rw`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.cons_val_zero as "Fin.cons_val_zero" in "Fin"

/-- `Fin.cons_val_succ a f i` states that `Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i`.

The later elements of a cons'd tuple come from the original tuple.

## When to use it
When you need to simplify `Fin.cons a f i.succ` to `f i` using `rw`.
-/
TheoremDoc Fin.cons_val_succ as "Fin.cons_val_succ" in "Fin"

NewTheorem Fin.cons_val_zero Fin.cons_val_succ

import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 6

Title "Dropping the Head"

Introduction "
# Fin.tail: Decomposing Tuples

If `Fin.cons` builds tuples by prepending, **`Fin.tail`**
decomposes them by dropping the first element.

Given a tuple `f : Fin (n + 1) → α`, `Fin.tail f` is the
tuple `Fin n → α` that skips the first element:

$$\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;f\\;(i) = f\\;(i + 1)$$

For example:
$$\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;\\texttt{![10, 20, 30]} = \\texttt{![20, 30]}$$

The first element `10` is dropped, and the remaining elements
`20, 30` form a shorter tuple.

**Your task**: Prove that dropping the head of `![a, b, c]`
gives `![b, c]`.
"

/-- `Fin.tail` drops the first element of a tuple. -/
Statement (a b c : ℕ) :
    Fin.tail (![a, b, c] : Fin 3 → ℕ) = ![b, c] := by
  Hint "`Fin.tail` of `![a, b, c]` is `![b, c]` by definition.
  Use `rfl`."
  rfl

Conclusion "
`Fin.cons` and `Fin.tail` are complementary operations:

- `Fin.cons` prepends: `Fin.cons a ![b, c] = ![a, b, c]`
- `Fin.tail` drops: `Fin.tail ![a, b, c] = ![b, c]`

Together, they give you the head and the tail of any tuple.
The head is `f 0` (just evaluate at index 0), and the tail is
`Fin.tail f`.

This decomposition — *every non-empty tuple is a head followed
by a tail* — is the tuple analog of how every non-empty list
is a head `::` tail. In the next level, you'll see the formal
identity that ties these pieces together.
"

/-- `Fin.tail f` drops the first element of tuple `f`.

## Syntax
```
Fin.tail f    -- drop the head
```

## Type
`Fin.tail : (Fin (n + 1) → α) → (Fin n → α)`

## Definition
`Fin.tail f i = f i.succ`

## Example
`Fin.tail ![10, 20, 30] = ![20, 30]`

## Key identity
`Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f` — every tuple equals
its head prepended to its tail (`Fin.cons_self_tail`).
-/
DefinitionDoc Fin.tail as "Fin.tail"

NewDefinition Fin.tail

input.content:
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 7

Title "Head-Tail Reconstruction"

Introduction "
# Every Tuple = Head + Tail

You've seen that `Fin.cons` prepends and `Fin.tail` drops.
The fundamental identity connecting them is:

$$\\texttt{Fin.cons}\\;(f\\;0)\\;(\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;f) = f$$

In words: if you take the head of `f` (which is `f 0`) and
prepend it to the tail of `f` (which is `Fin.tail f`), you
get back `f` itself.

This is **`Fin.cons_self_tail`**: `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.

This identity says that `cons` and `(head, tail)` are inverse
operations. Every tuple is completely determined by its head
and its tail.

In mathematics: if $v = (v_0, v_1, \\ldots, v_{n-1})$, then
$v = v_0 :: (v_1, \\ldots, v_{n-1})$ where $::$ is prepending.

**Your task**: Prove this identity.
"

/-- Every tuple equals its head prepended to its tail. -/
Statement (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) :
    (Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) : Fin 3 → ℕ) = f := by
  Hint "The theorem `Fin.cons_self_tail f` states exactly this:
  `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.
  Use `exact Fin.cons_self_tail f`."
  exact Fin.cons_self_tail f

Conclusion "
`Fin.cons_self_tail` is the key structural fact about tuples.
It says:

> Decomposing a tuple into head + tail and reassembling gives
> back the original tuple.

This will be essential later when you reason about tuples by
induction on length. The base case handles the empty tuple
(`Fin 0 → α`), and the inductive step decomposes
`Fin (n + 1) → α` into a head and a `Fin n → α` tail.
"

/-- `Fin.cons_self_tail f` states that
`Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.

Every tuple equals its head prepended to its tail.

## Syntax
```
exact Fin.cons_self_tail f  -- proves cons (f 0) (tail f) = f
```

## When to use it
When you need to decompose a tuple into its first element
and the remaining elements.

World "FinTuples"
Level 7

Title "Head-Tail Reconstruction"

Introduction "
# Every Tuple = Head + Tail

You've seen that `Fin.cons` prepends and `Fin.tail` drops.
The fundamental identity connecting them is:

$$\\texttt{Fin.cons}\\;(f\\;0)\\;(\\texttt{Fin.tail}\\;f) = f$$

In words: if you take the head of `f` (which is `f 0`) and
prepend it to the tail of `f` (which is `Fin.tail f`), you
get back `f` itself.

This is **`Fin.cons_self_tail`**: `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.

This identity says that `cons` and `(head, tail)` are inverse
operations. Every tuple is completely determined by its head
and its tail.

In mathematics: if $v = (v_0, v_1, \\ldots, v_{n-1})$, then
$v = v_0 :: (v_1, \\ldots, v_{n-1})$ where $::$ is prepending.

**Your task**: Prove this identity.
"

    (Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) : Fin 3 → ℕ) = f := by

  `Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f) = f`.
  Use `exact Fin.cons_self_tail f`."

induction on length. The base case handles the empty tuple
(`Fin 0 → α`), and the inductive step decomposes
`Fin (n + 1) → α` into a head and a `Fin n → α` tail.

import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 8

Title "Appending with vecSnoc"

Introduction "
# vecSnoc: Adding to the End

`Fin.cons` prepends an element at the *beginning* of a tuple.
What about adding an element at the *end*?

**`vecSnoc f x`** takes:
- A tuple `f : Fin n → α`
- An element `x : α`

and produces `vecSnoc f x : Fin (n + 1) → α` — the tuple `f`
with `x` appended at the end.

The access lemmas are:
- `vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x` — the last element is `x`
- `vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i` — earlier elements are from `f`

Notice the connection to FinNavigation: `Fin.cons` routes through
`0` / `succ` (the successor decomposition), while `vecSnoc` routes
through `castSucc` / `last` (the other decomposition you learned).
These are **dual constructions** — one builds from the front,
the other from the back.

**Your task**: Prove both access lemmas for `vecSnoc`.
"

/-- The access lemmas for vecSnoc. -/
Statement (a : ℕ) (f : Fin 2 → ℕ) :
    vecSnoc f a (Fin.last 2) = a ∧
    ∀ i : Fin 2, vecSnoc f a i.castSucc = f i := by
  Hint "Use `constructor` to split the conjunction."
  constructor
  · Hint "The goal is `vecSnoc f a (Fin.last 2) = a`.
    Use `exact vecSnoc_last f a`."
    exact vecSnoc_last f a
  · Hint "Start with `intro i`, then use
    `exact vecSnoc_castSucc f a i`."
    intro i
    exact vecSnoc_castSucc f a i

Conclusion "
You now have two ways to build tuples:

| Operation | Adds where | Routes through |
|---|---|---|
| `Fin.cons a f` | Beginning | `0` / `succ` |
| `vecSnoc f x` | End | `castSucc` / `last` |

The duality is exact:
- `Fin.cons` uses the **0/succ decomposition** from FinNavigation:
  every `Fin (n+1)` element is either `0` or `i.succ`
- `vecSnoc` uses the **castSucc/last decomposition**:
  every `Fin (n+1)` element is either `i.castSucc` or `Fin.last n`

This is the payoff from learning both decompositions in
FinNavigation — they power the two dual tuple constructors.
"

/-- `vecSnoc f x` appends element `x` to the end of tuple `f`,
producing a tuple of length `n + 1`.

## Syntax
```
vecSnoc f x    -- append x to the end of f
```

## Type
`vecSnoc : (Fin n → α) → α → Fin (n + 1) → α`

## Element access
- `vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x` — `vecSnoc_last`
- `vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i` — `vecSnoc_castSucc`

## Example
`vecSnoc ![10, 20] 30 = ![10, 20, 30]`
-/
DefinitionDoc vecSnoc as "vecSnoc"

/-- `vecSnoc_last f x` states that `vecSnoc f x (Fin.last n) = x`.

The last element of a snoc'd tuple is the appended element.
-/
TheoremDoc vecSnoc_last as "vecSnoc_last" in "Fin"

/-- `vecSnoc_castSucc f x i` states that `vecSnoc f x i.castSucc = f i`.

The earlier elements of a snoc'd tuple come from the original tuple.
-/
TheoremDoc vecSnoc_castSucc as "vecSnoc_castSucc" in "Fin"

NewDefinition vecSnoc
NewTheorem vecSnoc_last vecSnoc_castSucc

input.content:
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 9

Title "ext for Functions"

Introduction "
# Extensionality for Functions

In MeetFin, you used `ext` to prove two `Fin` elements are
equal by showing their underlying values are equal. Now you'll
use `ext` in a new context: **functions**.

For functions, extensionality says:

> Two functions are equal if and only if they agree on every input.

In symbols: $f = g \\iff \\forall\\, i,\\; f(i) = g(i)$.

When the goal is `f = g` where `f g : Fin n → α`, using
`ext i` introduces `i : Fin n` and changes the goal to `f i = g i`.

This is the same `ext` tactic, just applied to a different type.
In MeetFin, `ext` decomposed `Fin` equality into value equality.
Here, `ext` decomposes function equality into pointwise equality.

**Your task**: Prove `f = g` given that they agree on every input.
"

/-- Two functions that agree everywhere are equal. -/
Statement (f g : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h : ∀ i, f i = g i) :
    f = g := by
  Hint "Use `ext i` to introduce an index `i : Fin 3` and
  reduce the goal to `f i = g i`."
  ext i
  Hint "The goal is `f i = g i`. The hypothesis `{h}` says
  `∀ i, f i = g i`. Use `exact h i`."
  exact h i

Conclusion "
The pattern is simple:

1. `ext i` — reduce function equality to pointwise equality
2. Prove `f i = g i` for the introduced `i`

This is the same tactic you used in MeetFin for `Fin` elements.
Both are instances of the same principle: two composite objects
are equal when their components are equal.

Note: the tactic `funext i` does the same thing as `ext i`
for functions. Both are valid.
"

input.content:
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 10

Title "The Empty Tuple"

Introduction "
# There Is Only One Empty Tuple

What is `Fin 0 → ℕ`? It's a function from the *empty* type
`Fin 0` — a type with no elements.

Since there are no inputs, there are no choices to make: the
function has nothing to return. This means there is exactly
**one** function `Fin 0 → ℕ` (the empty function).

In tuple language: there is exactly one 0-tuple.

This is the tuple base case — analogous to the empty list `[]`.
Every inductive argument about tuples starts here.

The fact that `Fin 0` is empty also means something powerful:
*any* statement about all elements of `Fin 0` is vacuously true.
In Lean, `Fin.elim0 i` proves anything when `i : Fin 0`, because
no such `i` can ever exist.

**Your task**: Prove that any two functions `Fin 0 → ℕ` are equal.
Use `ext i` to introduce the index, then `exact Fin.elim0 i`
to eliminate the impossible case.
"

/-- Any two functions from Fin 0 are equal. -/
Statement (f g : Fin 0 → ℕ) : f = g := by
  Hint "Use `ext i` to introduce an index `i : Fin 0`."
  ext i
  Hint "The goal is `f i = g i` where `i : Fin 0`. But `Fin 0`
  has no elements! Use `exact Fin.elim0 i` to eliminate the
  impossible case."
  exact Fin.elim0 i

Conclusion "
`Fin.elim0 i` works because `i : Fin 0` is a value from the
empty type — it can never actually exist. Lean lets you prove
*anything* from an impossible hypothesis, and `Fin.elim0` is the
standard way to do this for `Fin 0`.

This is the tuple analog of the fact that the empty product
equals 1: there's exactly one way to make zero choices.

In the mathematical literature, this is sometimes written as
$\\alpha^0 = 1$ — the set of functions from the empty set to
$\\alpha$ has exactly one element.
"

input.content:
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 11

Title "Proving Tuple Equality"

Introduction "
# Identifying a Tuple from Its Values

Suppose you know the value of `f` at every index:
`f 0 = 1`, `f 1 = 4`, `f 2 = 9`. Can you conclude that
`f = ![1, 4, 9]`?

Yes — by function extensionality. The strategy:

1. `ext` to reduce `f = ![1, 4, 9]` to `f i = ![1, 4, 9] i`
2. Case-split on `i` to handle each index separately
3. At each concrete index, use the corresponding hypothesis
   and the fact that `![1, 4, 9]` reduces by definition

The case split is the same nested `cases` pattern you used
in MeetFin for `Fin 3`. After destructuring `i` as `⟨v, hv⟩`,
split on `v`: the cases `0`, `1`, `2` correspond to the three
hypotheses, and the impossible case `v ≥ 3` is closed by
`absurd hv (by omega)`.

**Your task**: Given the values of `f` at each index, prove
`f = ![1, 4, 9]`.
"

/-- A function is determined by its values at each index. -/
Statement (f : Fin 3 → ℕ)
    (h0 : f 0 = 1) (h1 : f 1 = 4) (h2 : f 2 = 9) :
    f = ![1, 4, 9] := by
  Hint "To show two functions are equal, check them index by index.
  Use `ext ⟨v, hv⟩` to introduce the index and
  reduce to `f ⟨v, hv⟩ = ![1, 4, 9] ⟨v, hv⟩`."
  ext ⟨v, hv⟩
  Hint "Now case-split on `v` with `cases v with | zero => ... | succ n => ...`.
  The `zero` case corresponds to index 0."
  cases v with
  | zero =>
    Hint "The goal is `f 0 = ![1, 4, 9] 0`, which simplifies to `f 0 = 1`.
    You have `{h0} : f 0 = 1`. Use `exact h0`."
    exact h0
  | succ n =>
    Hint (hidden := true) "Continue splitting: `cases n with | zero => ... | succ m => ...`"
    cases n with
    | zero =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "The goal is `f 1 = 4`. Use `exact h1`."
      exact h1
    | succ m =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "Split once more on `m`."
      cases m with
      | zero =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "The goal is `f 2 = 9`. Use `exact h2`."
        exact h2
      | succ k =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "Here `v = k + 3` but `hv` says `v < 3`.
        Impossible: `exact absurd hv (by omega)`."
        exact absurd hv (by omega)

Conclusion "
The `ext` + case split pattern is the standard way to prove
a function from `Fin n` equals a specific tuple:

```
ext ⟨v, hv⟩
cases v with
| zero => ...      -- handle index 0
| succ n =>
  cases n with
  | zero => ...    -- handle index 1
  | succ m => ...  -- handle index 2, etc.
```

The impossible branch (where `v ≥ n`) is always closed by
`exact absurd hv (by omega)`.

This pattern scales to any `Fin n`, though it gets verbose
for large `n`. (Later, you'll learn tactics like `fin_cases`
that automate this case split.)
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
DisabledTheorem funext

import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 12

Title "Computing with Tuples"

Introduction "
# Recognizing a Computed Tuple

You now know how to verify that a function equals a specific
tuple when given its values as hypotheses. What about when
the function is defined by a formula?

Consider `fun (i : Fin 3) => i.val ^ 2`. This computes:
- At `i = 0`: $0^2 = 0$
- At `i = 1`: $1^2 = 1$
- At `i = 2`: $2^2 = 4$

So this function should equal `![0, 1, 4]`.

The proof strategy is the same: `ext` to reduce to pointwise
equality, then case-split. At each concrete index, both sides
compute to the same number, so `rfl` closes the goal.

**Your task**: Prove that squaring gives `![0, 1, 4]`.
"

/-- The squaring function on Fin 3, expressed as a tuple. -/
Statement :
    (fun (i : Fin 3) => i.val ^ 2) = ![0, 1, 4] := by
  Hint "Use `ext ⟨v, hv⟩` to introduce the index, then case-split on `v`."
  ext ⟨v, hv⟩
  Hint (hidden := true) "`cases v with | zero => rfl | succ n => ...`"
  cases v with
  | zero => rfl
  | succ n =>
    Hint (hidden := true) "`cases n with | zero => rfl | succ m => ...`"
    cases n with
    | zero => rfl
    | succ m =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "Split on `m`, then `rfl` or `absurd`."
      cases m with
      | zero => rfl
      | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)

Conclusion "
When both sides of the equality are computable, each case
reduces to a concrete equation that `rfl` handles.

The general pattern for showing `fun i => expr i = ![a, b, c]`:
1. `ext` to introduce the index
2. Case-split to reach a concrete index
3. `rfl` at each concrete case (both sides evaluate to the same number)
4. `absurd` for the impossible case
"

input.content:
import Game.Metadata
import Mathlib.Data.Fin.VecNotation

World "FinTuples"
Level 13

Title "Boss: Tuple Reconstruction"

Introduction "
# Boss: Reconstructing a Tuple

Time to combine the tuple moves you've learned in a single proof.

You're given a function `f : Fin 3 → ℕ` along with two facts:
- `h0 : f 0 = 1` — the head is 1
- `ht : Fin.tail f = ![2, 3]` — the tail is `![2, 3]`

From these, you must prove `f = ![1, 2, 3]`.

**Strategy**: Use `Fin.cons_self_tail` to express `f` as
`Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f)`, then substitute the hypotheses.

1. `have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm` — this gives
   `key : f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f)`
2. `rw [h0, ht] at key` — substitutes to get
   `key : f = Fin.cons 1 ![2, 3]` (which is `f = ![1, 2, 3]`)
3. `exact key` — close the goal

Alternatively, you can use `ext` + case split — the approach
from Level 11. Both strategies work.
"

/-- Reconstruct a tuple from its head and tail. -/
Statement (f : Fin 3 → ℕ) (h0 : f 0 = 1) (ht : Fin.tail f = ![2, 3]) :
    f = ![1, 2, 3] := by
  Hint "Use `Fin.cons_self_tail` to decompose `f`:
  `have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm`
  This gives `key : f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f)`."
  Hint (hidden := true) "Alternative: use `ext ⟨v, hv⟩` and
  case-split. For the `zero` case, use `h0`. For the `succ` cases,
  use `congr_fun ht` to derive from the tail hypothesis."
  have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm
  Hint "Now substitute the hypotheses into `key`:
  `rw [h0, ht] at key`."
  rw [h0, ht] at key
  Hint "Now `key` says `f = ![1, 2, 3]`. Use `exact key`."
  exact key

  Branch
    ext ⟨v, hv⟩
    cases v with
    | zero => exact h0
    | succ n =>
      exact congr_fun ht ⟨n, by omega⟩

Conclusion "
Congratulations — you've completed **Fin Tuples**!

Here's your tuple toolkit:

| Tool | What it does | Learned in |
|---|---|---|
| `![a, b, c]` | Build tuple with vec notation | Levels 1-2 |
| `fun \\| 0 => a \\| 1 => b \\| ...` | Build tuple by cases | Level 3 |
| `Fin.cons a f` | Prepend element to tuple | Level 4 |
| `Fin.cons_val_zero` | `Fin.cons a f 0 = a` | Level 5 |
| `Fin.cons_val_succ` | `Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i` | Level 5 |
| `Fin.tail f` | Drop the first element | Level 6 |
| `Fin.cons_self_tail f` | `f = cons (f 0) (tail f)` | Level 7 |
| `vecSnoc f x` | Append element to tuple | Level 8 |
| `vecSnoc_last` / `vecSnoc_castSucc` | Access snoc'd elements | Level 8 |
| `ext i` | Reduce function equality to pointwise | Level 9 |
| `Fin.elim0 i` | Eliminate impossible `Fin 0` | Level 10 |
| `ext` + case split | Prove `f = ![...]` by index | Levels 11-12 |

**The big picture**: `Fin n → α` models ordered tuples. The
notation `![a, b, c]` makes them concrete. `Fin.cons` builds from
the front, `vecSnoc` from the back. The `cons`/`tail`
decomposition lets you reason about tuples inductively.
Function extensionality (`ext`) lets you prove two tuples
are equal by checking them index by index.

**Where this is going**: Now that you can build and manipulate
tuples, you're ready for the Pset (problem set) world, which
will test these skills on fresh problems without scaffolding.
"

import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L01_FunctionsAsTuples
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L02_VecNotation
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L03_BuildingFunctions
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L04_PrependingWithCons
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L05_ConsAccessLemmas
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L06_DroppingTheHead
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L07_HeadTailReconstruction
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L08_AppendingWithSnoc
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L09_ExtForFunctions
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L10_TheEmptyTuple
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L11_ProvingTupleEquality
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L12_ComputingWithTuples
import Game.Levels.FinTuples.L13_Boss

World "FinTuples"
Title "Fin Tuples"

Introduction "
# Fin Tuples: Functions as Ordered Data

You've learned that `Fin n` is the finite index set
$\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n-1\\}$, and you can navigate within it using
`last`, `castSucc`, and `succ`. Now comes the payoff:

**A function `Fin n → α` is an `n`-tuple.**

This is one of the most useful identifications in formalized
mathematics. A vector in $\\mathbb{R}^3$ is a function
`Fin 3 → ℝ`. A sequence of length 5 is a function
`Fin 5 → α`. The entries of an $n \\times m$ matrix are a
function `Fin n → Fin m → ℝ`.

The advantage of using functions instead of lists: the length
is part of the type. You can't accidentally add a 3-vector to
a 4-vector — the types don't match.

In this world, you'll learn to:
- **Build** tuples using `![a, b, c]` notation and `Fin.cons`
- **Append** elements using `vecSnoc`
- **Access** elements using named lemmas
- **Decompose** tuples into head + tail using `Fin.tail`
- **Prove** two tuples are equal using function extensionality (`ext`)

The central tool is **`ext`**: two tuples are equal if and only
if they agree at every index. You already know `ext` for `Fin`
elements (two `Fin` values are equal when their natural numbers
are equal). Here, `ext` says two functions are equal when they
agree on all inputs — same tactic, new context.

The boss at the end will ask you to reconstruct a tuple from
its head and tail, combining decomposition with extensionality
in a single proof.
"

Title "Fin Tuples"

Introduction "
# Fin Tuples: Functions as Ordered Data

You've learned that `Fin n` is the finite index set
$\\{0, 1, \\ldots, n-1\\}$, and you can navigate within it using

- **Build** tuples using `![a, b, c]` notation and `Fin.cons`
- **Access** elements by evaluation (index the function)
- **Decompose** tuples into head + tail using `Fin.tail`
- **Prove** two tuples are equal using function extensionality (`ext`)
